Codeforces Round 1073 (Div. 1, Div. 2) Editorial
By nifeshe, 2 months ago,

Special thanks to reirugan, weirdflexbutok and Intellegent for helping with editorials.

2191A - Array Coloring

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: reirugan
Hints
Hint 1

Without the "after sorting condition", there are only two ways to color the cards.

Solution

Without the "after sorting condition", there are only two ways to color the cards. We must have that aa is alternating in color.

Without loss of generality, color the odd indices red and the even indices blue. This uniquely colors every card. Now, let's sort the array aa, while also keeping track of the colors; one way to do this is by sorting it as a vector of pairs. Then we simply have to verify that no two adjacent cards are the same color.

Alternatively, you can use the fact that aa is a permutation, so all differences of ai−iai−i have to either be odd or even.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for(auto &i : a) {
        cin >> i;
    }

    int cnt = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cnt += (a[i] % 2 != (i + 1) % 2);
    }

    cout << (cnt == 0 || cnt == n? "YES" : "NO") << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 139 Good Problem
  • 54 Okay Problem
  • 13 Bad Problem
  • 5 Didn't Solve

2191B - MEX Reordering

  • Idea: satyam343
  • Solution: satyam343
  • Editorial: reirugan
Hints
Hint 1

If there are no zeros in aa, it's impossible. If there's exactly one zero, it's always possible.

Hint 2

If there are at least 22 zeros and no ones, there's a split point between the two zeros.

Hint 3

If there are at least 22 zeros and a one, you can put all zeros at the end.

Solution

Notice that f(l,r)>0f(l,r)>0 if and only if a[l,r]a[l,r] contains a zero. Therefore, if aa does not contain any zeros, the answer is NONO, because all values of f(l,r)f(l,r) are zero, so they are all equal.

Conversely, consider the case where there is exactly one zero. Then we can simply place this zero at the very end. Now, for any 1≤i≤n−11≤i≤n−1, we have that f(1,i)=0f(1,i)=0, but f(i+1,n)≠0f(i+1,n)≠0, so the answer is YESYES.

Finally, consider the case where there are at least two zeros. If aa does not contain any ones, then the answer is NONO. This is because we can always choose an ii that splits the zeros — that is, a[1,i]a[1,i] and a[i+1,n]a[i+1,n] both contain a zero — then we will have that f(1,i)=f(i+1,n)=1f(1,i)=f(i+1,n)=1.

Otherwise, if aa does contain a one, then we can place all of the zeros at the very end. Now, consider any 1≤i≤n−11≤i≤n−1. If a[1,i]a[1,i] does not contain a zero, then f(1,i)=0f(1,i)=0, but f(i+1,n)≠0f(i+1,n)≠0. If a[1,i]a[1,i] does contain a zero, then it also contains a one (because all ones are before all zeros), so f(1,i)≥2f(1,i)≥2. But a[i+1,n]a[i+1,n] consists of only zeros, so f(i+1,n)=1f(i+1,n)=1. Therefore, the answer is YESYES.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for(auto &i : a) {
        cin >> i;
    }

    vector<int> cnt(n + 1);
    for(auto i : a) cnt[i]++;

    if(cnt[0] == 0) cout << "NO\n";
    else if(cnt[1] > 0) cout << "YES\n";
    else cout << (cnt[0] == 1? "YES" : "NO") << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 183 Good Problem
  • 30 Okay Problem
  • 21 Bad Problem
  • 14 Didn't Solve

2190A - Sorting Game

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: reirugan
Hints
Hint 1

Let tt be the sorted version of ss. Then if s=ts=t at any point, the player to move loses, because there is no legal move remaining.

Hint 2

It's possible to sort in one move.

Solution

Let tt be the sorted version of ss. Then if s=ts=t at any point, the player to move loses, because there is no legal move remaining.

If s=ts=t at the start of the game, the answer is BobBob. Otherwise, we claim the answer is AliceAlice, and she can win on the first turn by choosing all of the indices at which ss and tt differ.

First, we claim this is non-increasing. Let ii be the number of zeros in ss, so t[1,i]t[1,i] consists of only zeros, and t[i+1,n]t[i+1,n] consists of only ones. Then for any jj between 11 and ii, if sjsj and tjtj differ then sj=1sj=1. Similarly, for any jj between i+1i+1 and nn, if sjsj and tjtj differ then sj=0sj=0. So out of the chosen indices, the indices between 11 and ii satisfy sj=1sj=1, and the indices between i+1i+1 and nn satisfy sj=0sj=0, so the sequence is non-increasing.

Next, we claim that this sorts the array; in particular, this implies that it changes the array, so it is legal. Furthermore, this wins immediately since Bob has no subsequent move. Indeed, we see that the number of chosen indices between 11 and ii is equal to the number of chosen indices between i+1i+1 and nn, because we must have that the number of zeros and ones in ss and tt are equal. So the operation simply exchanges all of the ones in s[1,i]s[1,i] with all of the zeros in s[i+1,n]s[i+1,n], thus making s=ts=t as desired.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    string s;
    cin >> s;

    string sorted = s;
    sort(sorted.begin(), sorted.end());

    if (s == sorted) {
        cout << "Bob\n";
        return;
    }

    vector<int> idx;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        if(s[i] != sorted[i]) {
            idx.push_back(i + 1);
        }
    }

    cout << "Alice\n";
    cout << idx.size() << '\n';
    for(auto i : idx) cout << i << " ";
    cout << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 186 Good Problem
  • 39 Okay Problem
  • 63 Bad Problem
  • 22 Didn't Solve

2190B1 - Sub-RBS (Easy Version)

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: weirdflexbutok
Hints
Hint 1

Try to fix the first position where the better sequence and string differ in characters.

Hint 2

Greedily delete characters. You will always end up with an RBS if possible it's possible to make the total balance zero.

Solution

For a string tt to be better than ss, note that some prefix of tt will be identical to prefix of ss, then at the position ii where ti≠siti≠si, si=si= )) and ti=ti= ((.

Consider the following greedy algorithm. We try to fix the first position where ti≠siti≠si and si=si= )), therefore we have t[1:i−1]t[1:i−1] = s[1:i−1]s[1:i−1]. Now sisi must be )). To make tt bigger, we look for the first index j>ij>i such that sjsj = ((. Since this is the next character we add to the subsequence, we must skip the last j−ij−i characters, which will all be )). For a sequence to be an RBS, it is necessary for it to have equal number of (( and )). Therefore, we must atleast skip j−ij−i more ( after position jj in tt.

We can show that if we can have j−ij−i (( brackets after position jj and we delete them, we will always end up with RBS of size n−2×(j−i)n−2×(j−i).

Proof

Define a balance of a prefix of a bracket string as the number of opening brackets minus the number of closing brackets.

For a string tt to be an RBS, all of its prefix balances must be non-negative. ss is an RBS. We can show that for any index in tt, balt[i]≥bals[j]balt[i]≥bals[j], where the jj-th character in ss is at the ii-th index in tt. This is because deleting an )) in ss will increase the balance of every index after the position. Since we only delete (( after )), all the indices' balances must be greater or equal to what it was before.

Therefore, we can simple precompute the next position of (( for each index, and find the max over n−2×(j−i)n−2×(j−i) as long as there are sufficient )) to delete after jj.

Time complexity : O(n)O(n)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    std::cin.tie(0);

    int T;
    cin >> T;
    while(T--){
        int n;
        string s;
        cin >> n >> s;
        vector<int> nx(n + 1, n + 1);
        vector<int> suf(n + 1, 0);
        for(int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--){
            if(s[i] == '(') nx[i] = i;
            else nx[i] = nx[i + 1];
            if(s[i] == '(') suf[i] += 1;
            suf[i] += suf[i + 1];
        }

        int ans = -1;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            if(s[i] == ')' and nx[i] <= n){
                int ig = nx[i] - i;
                if(suf[nx[i] + 1] >= ig){
                    ans = max(ans, n - 2 * ig);
                }
            }
        }

        cout << ans << '\n';
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 155 Good Problem
  • 52 Okay Problem
  • 44 Bad Problem
  • 27 Didn't Solve

2190B2 - Sub-RBS (Hard Version)

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: weirdflexbutok
Hints
Hint 1

What values can f(t)f(t) take if length of tt is fixed?

Answer

00 or |t|−2|t|−2

Hint 2

What is a simple condition the string must satisfy for f(t)f(t) to be |t|−2|t|−2?

Answer

tt must be an RBS, and )(()(( must be a subsequence of tt.

Hint 3

Use dynamic programming.

Solution

First, read the solution to D1.

We can further observe that if our answer is n−2×kn−2×k, we must have had (((((( ⋯⋯ ()() followed by kk )) s. If we instead follow the same greedy algorithm at the index of the final ((, we end up getting k=1k=1. Therefore, if a good subsequence exists, we can always get a subsequence of length n−2n−2.

Therefore, a string has a better subsequence of length n−2n−2 iff it has a ")()(" followed by "((". We can further relax this condition to ss is good if ss is a RBS and it has ")(()((" as a subsequence.

Proof

Suppose ss has )(()(( at position i<j<ki<j<k. If s[i+1]=s[i+1]= ((, we can choose (i,i+1,k)(i,i+1,k) and we are done. Else, (i+1,j,k)(i+1,j,k) is the same subsequence. Repeating this, we will always end up with (l,l+1,k)(l,l+1,k) eventually for some ll.

Now, we can simply count the number of subsequences of different lengths that are an RBS and have a subsequence ")(()((". This can be done by dynamic programming similar to counting number of subsequences that are RBS.

We define the following DP state:

dp[i][b][l][k]dp[i][b][l][k] = number of bracket sequences using first ii characters, with balance bb, having length ll and kk indicates how many characters of ")(()((" occur as a subsequence in the strings.

We obtain the following cases:

  • If s[i]=s[i]= (( and k=k= {1,21,2}, dp[i][b+1][l+1][k+1]dp[i][b+1][l+1][k+1] +=dp[i−1][b][l][k]+=dp[i−1][b][l][k]

  • If s[i]=s[i]= (( and k=k= {0,30,3}, dp[i][b+1][l+1][k]dp[i][b+1][l+1][k] +=dp[i−1][b][l][k]+=dp[i−1][b][l][k]

  • If s[i]=s[i]= )) and k=k= {00} and b≥1b≥1, dp[i][b−1][l+1][k+1]dp[i][b−1][l+1][k+1] +=dp[i−1][b][l][k]+=dp[i−1][b][l][k]

  • If s[i]=s[i]= )) and k=k= {1,2,31,2,3} and b≥1b≥1, dp[i][b−1][l+1][k]dp[i][b−1][l+1][k] +=dp[i−1][b][l][k]+=dp[i−1][b][l][k]

We finally output answer as the sum over dp[n][0][l][3]×(l−2)dp[n][0][l][3]×(l−2) over all ll.

Time Complexity: O(n3)O(n3). This can also be implemented in O(n2)O(n2) by not storing the length but the sum of lengths and the count separately.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

// Mint template omitted for readability

constexpr int md = 998244353;
using Mint = Modular<std::integral_constant<decay<decltype(md)>::type, md>>;

vector<Mint> fact(1, 1);
vector<Mint> inv_fact(1, 1);

Mint C(int n, int k) {
    if (k < 0 || k > n) {
        return 0;
    }
    while ((int) fact.size() < n + 1) {
        fact.push_back(fact.back() * (int) fact.size());
        inv_fact.push_back(1 / fact.back());
    }
    return fact[n] * inv_fact[k] * inv_fact[n - k];
}

int main(){
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    std::cin.tie(0);
    int T;
    cin >> T;
    while(T--){
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        string s;
        cin >> s;
        string t = ")((";
        vector<vector<vector<Mint>>> dp(4, vector(n + 1, vector(n + 1, Mint(0)))); // balance, length
        dp[0][0][0] = 1;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            auto ndp = dp;
            int add = s[i] == '(' ? 1 : -1;
            for(int bal = 0; bal < n; bal++){
                for(int len = 0; len < n; len++){
                    if(bal + add < 0) continue;
                    for(int ii = 0; ii < 4; ii++){
                        int ni = ii;
                        if(ii == 0 and s[i] == ')') ni++;
                        if((ii == 1 or ii == 2) and s[i] == '(') ni++;
                        ndp[ni][bal + add][len + 1] += dp[ii][bal][len];
                    }
                }
            }
            swap(dp, ndp);
        }

        Mint ans = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i <= n; i++){
            ans += dp[3][0][i] * (i - 2);
        }

        cout << ans << '\n';
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 120 Good Problem
  • 20 Okay Problem
  • 28 Bad Problem
  • 27 Didn't Solve

2190C - Comparable Permutations

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: reirugan
Hints
Hint 1

q>pq>p and rev(q)>rev(p)rev(q)>rev(p) is equivalent to the following condition: let ii be the first index at which pp and qq differ, and let jj be the last index at which pp and qq differ. Then we must have qi>piqi>pi and qj>pjqj>pj.

Hint 2

Fix some i<ji<j, and consider the subarray p[i,j]p[i,j]. We can rearrange these elements however we want, as long as we increase both endpoints. It is easy to see that this is impossible if pipi or pjpj is the maximum of the subarray. Otherwise, we want to choose the lexicographically smallest such option. Set qiqi to be the smallest element in p[i,j]p[i,j] that is larger than pipi. Then, we place the rest of the elements in ascending order to fill q[i+1,j]q[i+1,j].

Hint 3

Optimal i=k−1i=k−1, where kk is the largest index where pk>pk−1pk>pk−1 and pk>pk+1pk>pk+1.

Hint 4

[k,n][k,n] is first decreasing, then increasing.

Solution

We require that q>pq>p and rev(q)>rev(p)rev(q)>rev(p). By definition, this is equivalent to the following condition: let ii be the first index at which pp and qq differ, and let jj be the last index at which pp and qq differ. Then we must have that qi>piqi>pi and qj>pjqj>pj.

Now, fix some i<ji<j, and consider the subarray p[i,j]p[i,j]. We can rearrange these elements however we want, as long as we increase both endpoints. It is easy to see that this is impossible if pipi or pjpj is the maximum of the subarray. Otherwise, we want to choose the lexicographically smallest such option. To do so, we set qiqi to be the smallest element in p[i,j]p[i,j] that is larger than pipi. Then, we place the rest of the elements in ascending order to fill q[i+1,j]q[i+1,j].

Let's verify that this is valid. Clearly, qi>piqi>pi by construction. Now, qjqj is the maximum of q[i+1,j]q[i+1,j], so either qjqj or qiqi is the maximum of p[i,j]p[i,j]. If qjqj is the maximum, then it must be larger than pjpj because pjpj is not the maximum. Otherwise, if qiqi is the maximum, then pipi is the second maximum by construction, so qj=piqj=pi. Thus, qjqj is larger than pjpj because pjpj is not the maximum or the second maximum.

We conclude that any choice of i<ji<j such that pipi and pjpj are not the maximum of p[i,j]p[i,j] yields a valid choice of qq, and the optimal one is the one produced by the above construction.

Now, we want to find the choice of ii and jj that yield the lexicographically smallest option. It is easy to see that we want to use the largest possible value of ii, because we are going to keep everything before index ii the same, and increase the value at index ii. Let 1<k<n1<k<n denote the index of the last local maximum; that is, kk is the largest integer satisfying pk>pk−1,pk+1pk>pk−1,pk+1. Then we claim that i=k−1i=k−1 is optimal.

First, we show that it is valid. Indeed, the subarray p[i,i+2]=p[k−1,k+1]p[i,i+2]=p[k−1,k+1] is clearly valid. Next, we show that there is no larger valid choice of ii. Notice that p[k,n]p[k,n] is first decreasing, then increasing (possibly over an empty subarray). If we choose i<ji<j contained in the decreasing portion, then pipi will be the maximum of p[i,j]p[i,j]. If we choose i<ji<j in the increasing portion, then pjpj will be the maximum of p[i,j]p[i,j]. Finally, if we choose i<ji<j that crosses from the decreasing to the increasing portion, then max(pi,pj)max(pi,pj) is the maximum of p[i,j]p[i,j]. Therefore, no subarray that uses i≥ki≥k can possibly be valid.

We conclude that we must choose i=k−1i=k−1. Notice that identical reasoning to the above proof shows that if there is no local maximum, then no valid qq exists, so we may output −1−1. We further observe that the value of kk, if it exists, can be determined in n−1n−1 queries of adjacent values.

Now, we want to choose the value of jj. It is easy to see that we want to choose the largest possible value of jj, because it gives us strictly more options of how to rearrange the subarray. Since p[k,n]p[k,n] is decreasing then (possibly) increasing, it suffices to find the first value larger than pkpk, then we will choose the subarray to end right before that value. If no such value exists, we can choose j=nj=n.

However, we do not have to explicitly find the value of jj. In fact, it suffices to pretend that j=nj=n, and then using the construction from earlier, it will naturally follow that everything larger than the actual value of jj will remain untouched, since that portion of pp is already increasing. So now, we simply have to sort the values in p[i,n]p[i,n], then move the one that comes directly after pipi to the front.

Notice that we can split this subarray into three monotonic sections; furthermore, we know where the cutoffs for these sections are, since we have queried all adjacent values before. We can sort by finding the next minimum of the subarray one by one. We know that the next minimum must be the next minimum of one of the three sections, and since they are monotonic, we can track these with three pointers. We can find which of these three values is the smallest using two queries. In total, we sort the subarray using at most 2(n−1)2(n−1) queries. We can then move the value directly after pipi to the spot qiqi without any additional queries.

This solves the problem using at most 3n3n queries, as desired.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;

    auto ask = [&](int i, int j) {
        cout << "? " << i << " " << j << endl;

        int ans;
        cin >> ans;
        return ans;
    };

    int j = -1;
    vector<int> val(n + 2); val[n] = 1;
    for(int i = n - 1; i; i--) {
        val[i] = ask(i, i + 1);
        if(val[i] && !val[i + 1]) {
            j = i;
            break;
        }
    }
    if(j == -1) {
        cout << "! " << -1 << endl;
        return;
    }

    // Need to sort [j + 1, n]
    int k = j + 1;
    while(!val[k + 1]) k++;

    // [j + 1, k] - decreasing
    // [k + 1, n] - increasing
    vector<int> order;
    int l = k, r = k + 1;
    while(l > j && r <= n) {
        if(ask(l, r)) order.push_back(l--);
        else order.push_back(r++);
    }
    while(l > j) order.push_back(l--);
    while(r <= n) order.push_back(r++);

    l = 0, r = order.size() - 1;
    while(l <= r) {
        int m = l + r >> 1;
        if(ask(j, order[m])) r = m - 1;
        else l = m + 1;
    }
    assert(l < order.size());

    vector<int> res(n + 1);
    iota(res.begin(), res.end(), 0);
    res[j] = order[l];
    order[l] = j;
    for(int i = j + 1; i <= n; i++) {
        res[i] = order[i - j - 1];
    }

    res.erase(res.begin());
    cout << "! "; for(auto i : res) cout << i << " "; cout << endl;
}

signed main() {
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 106 Good Problem
  • 6 Okay Problem
  • 12 Bad Problem
  • 5 Didn't Solve

2190D - Prufer Vertex

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: Intellegent, nifeshe
Hints
Hint 1

Why isn't P(v)P(v) always n−1n−1? Specifically, when can we delete vertex n−1n−1?

Hint 2

Since each tree (with n≥2n≥2) has at least two leaves, n−1n−1 can be deleted only if n−1n−1 and nn are the only leaves. How to find P(v)P(v) using this fact?

Hint 3

This means that there's just a single path from nn to n−1n−1. In this case it's obvious that the second vertex on the path will be left at the end. Therefore, P(v)P(v) is always the second vertex on the path from nn to n−1n−1.

Hint 4

To count this you really only need to consider the case when vv and nn are adjacent.

Hint 5

All vertices in a component are indistinguishable.

Solution

Why isn't P(v)P(v) always n−1n−1? Specifically, when can we delete vertex n−1n−1?

Since each tree (with n≥2n≥2) has at least two leaves, n−1n−1 can be deleted only if n−1n−1 and nn are the only leaves. This means that there's just a single path from nn to n−1n−1. In this case it's obvious that the second vertex on the path will be left at the end. Therefore, P(v)P(v) is always the second vertex on the path from nn to n−1n−1.

So now we just have to count the number of ways to connect the tree satisfying these conditions. The easiest case is when nn and n−1n−1 are in the same component, then the Prufer vertex is fixed, so making the forest a tree doesn't change it.

Let's assume that nn and vv are already connected, because if not, we have to connect them with an edge, and we can update all the needed values dynamically.

We have to connect the tree so that n−1n−1 is in the subtree of vv (when rooted at nn).

Let CC be the set of vertices in the component of nn. Notice that since all vertices in CC are indistinguishable, the component with n−1n−1 will be attached to the component of nn to each vertex in CC in the same number of final trees.

Let kk be the size of the subtree of vv. Then in k|C|k|C| of all trees n−1n−1 will be connected to nn through a subtree of vv.

Therefore, we just need to maintain the total number of ways to complete the tree and after adding edges from nn to vv, and multiply that value by k|C|k|C| to solve the problem.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n) or O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

const int mod = 998244353;

// Removed Mint template for better readability

vector<Mint> get(vector<vector<int>> g) {
    int n = g.size() - 1;

    vector<int> comp(n + 1, -1), dp(n + 1, 1), parent(n + 1), sizes(n);
    int k = 0;
    function<void(int, int)> dfs = [&](int v, int p) {
        comp[v] = k; sizes[k]++;

        for(auto to : g[v]) {
            if(to == p) continue;

            parent[to] = v;
            dfs(to, v);
            dp[v] += dp[to];
        }
    };
    for(int v = n; v; v--) {
        if(comp[v] == -1) {
            dfs(v, 0);
            k++;
        }
    }

    vector<int> sz(n + 1);
    for(int v = 1; v <= n; v++) sz[v] = sizes[comp[v]];

    vector<Mint> inv(n + 1); inv[1] = 1;
    for(int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        inv[i] = mod - inv[mod % i] * (mod / i);
    }

    vector<Mint> ans(n);
    Mint p = 1;
    for(int i = 0; i < k; i++) p *= sizes[i];

    vector<Mint> n_power(n + 1);
    n_power[0] = 1; for(int i = 1; i <= n; i++) n_power[i] = n_power[i - 1] * n;

    auto n_pow = [&](int k) {
        if(k >= 0) return n_power[k];
        else if(k == -1) return inv[n];
        else assert(0);
    };

    if(comp[n] == comp[n - 1]) {
        // If n and n - 1 are already in one component P(T) is determined
        int v = n - 1;
        while(parent[v] != n) v = parent[v];

        ans[v] = p * n_pow(k - 2);
    }
    else {
        for(int v = 1; v <= n - 1; v++) {
            if(comp[v] == comp[n]) {
                // v and n have to be connected
                if(parent[v] != n) continue;

                ans[v] = dp[v] * p * inv[sz[n]] * n_pow(k - 2);
            }
            else if(comp[v] == comp[n - 1]) {
                // Just connect v and n
                ans[v] = p * inv[sz[n]] * inv[sz[n - 1]] * (sz[n] + sz[n - 1]) * n_pow(k - 3);
            }
            else {
                // Connect v and n and same as first case
                ans[v] = sz[v] * p * inv[sz[n]] * inv[sz[v]] * n_pow(k - 3);
            }
        }
    }

    return ans;
}

void solve() {
    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;

    vector<vector<int>> g(n + 1);
    for(int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;

        g[u].push_back(v); g[v].push_back(u);
    }

    vector<Mint> ans = get(g);
    for(int v = 1; v < n; v++) cout << ans[v] << ' '; cout << endl;
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 67 Good Problem
  • 13 Okay Problem
  • 4 Bad Problem
  • 3 Didn't Solve

2190E - Median Permutation

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: nifeshe

Special thanks to Sana for showing me how to go from O(n2)O(n2) to O(n)O(n).

Hints
Hint 1

If you bruteforce and oeis, you'll find out that the total number of valid permutations is n⋅2n−2n⋅2n−2. You should forget about this, trying to prove this directly is not gonna work.

Hint 2

Consider the case where p1=1p1=1 and pn=npn=n.

Hint 3

Look at even and odd indices separately.

Hint 4

When p1=1p1=1 and pn=npn=n, the condition is that pi−2<pipi−2<pi must hold for all ii. Try to generalize this condition for other cases.

Hint 5

If pi−2<pi>pi+2pi−2<pi>pi+2, we must have pi=npi=n, so even and odd indices are almost monotonic.

Hint 6

Consider a case where p1<p3p1<p3 and p2>p4p2>p4 and the restrictions this puts on the problem.

Hint 7

The two cases above are sufficient, try to make them easier two count.

Hint 8

There are 22 chains of increasing values.

Solution

Notice that 11 and nn can never be medians, so f(p)f(p) has to be a permutation of 2,…,n2,…,n.

Suppose that we have 5 consecutive elements [p1,p2,p3,p4,p5][p1,p2,p3,p4,p5], and p1<p3>p5p1<p3>p5. WLOG, assume that p2<p4p2<p4. It turns out there's a very tight condition for this case to not have duplicates. Mainly, p3p3 has to be the largest element.

Proof

Suppose p4p4 is the largest element instead. Median of [3,5][3,5] is p3p3. For [2,4][2,4] to not have p3p3 as the median, we must have p3<p2<p4p3<p2<p4, and the median of [2,4][2,4] is p2p2. But then in [1,3][1,3] we have p1<p3<p2p1<p3<p2, so p3p3 is the median again, which is a contradiction.

Notice that since p3p3 is the largest element among all 33 subarrays in which it takes part in, p3p3 cannot appear in f(p)f(p). Therefore, an important observation is: if pi−2<pi>pi+2pi−2<pi>pi+2, then pi=npi=n. Similarly, if pi−2>pi<pi+2pi−2>pi<pi+2, then pi=1pi=1.

From this we can see that all indices with the same parity are almost monotonic. But how do even and odd indices interact with each other?

Let's consider another case: [p1,p2,p3,p4][p1,p2,p3,p4], p1<p3p1<p3 and p2>p4p2>p4 (odd is increasing, even is decreasing). We can see that this case also has a simple structure. Mainly, if we bruteforce (to not consider a lot of cases) all valid permutations of size 44 that satisfy the two inequalities above, we'll get only two solutions: [1,4,2,3][1,4,2,3] and [3,2,4,1][3,2,4,1]. This means that either all even indices have values greater than all odd indices, or vice versa.

The two conditions we found are actually already sufficient, and it's very easy to prove this.

Suppose f(p)i=f(p)jf(p)i=f(p)j with i<ji<j, so pp is invalid. Notice that for this to happen [i,i+2][i,i+2] and [j,j+2][j,j+2] have to both include the same value in pp, so [i,i+2][i,i+2] and [j,j+2][j,j+2] have to intersect, so i+2≥ji+2≥j, or j−i≤2j−i≤2. Therefore, it is sufficient to check that there are no "bad" subarrays [i,i+4][i,i+4], and if all subarrays of size 55 are valid in pp, pp is valid as well.

Using this and our two conditions above we can just check all permutations of size 55 and verify that they are sufficient.

So now we only have to understand how to count. The conditions we have are actually pretty restrictive.

WLOG, assume that 11 is located earlier than nn.

First consider the case where pos1pos1 and posnposn have the different parity.

Consider the picture below. Each arrow denotes that the element should be smaller than the element arrow points to. Let's see how even and odd indices compare to each other.

Spoiler

Look at i=2i=2. We have p2>p4p2>p4 and p3<p5p3<p5, which is the second condition we had. Since p4=1p4=1, we must have p4<p2<p3<p5p4<p2<p3<p5. For i=1i=1 the same thing holds, and since we already know that p3>p2p3>p2 (and all odd indices in this case must have a larger element than even indices), we must have p4<p2<p1<p3<p5p4<p2<p1<p3<p5.

The same thing happens after nn on the right side, we must have: p10<p12<p13<p11<p9p10<p12<p13<p11<p9, so essentially can add two new (purple) arrows to the picture:

Spoiler

And after some recoloring, we can see that the when 11 and nn are located on different parities, there must be two chains of values:

Spoiler

After applying the same logic, we get a similar situation when 11 and nn are located on the same parity:

Spoiler

From this we can see that in all cases there are at most two increasing chains (dark-red and orange on the two pictures above).

Since we know the positions of 11 and nn, the problem now transformed into: given two chains of increasing values, we have to count the number of ways to put all values 1,2,…,n1,2,…,n into the chains with some values already being placed.

Suppose we have two chains aa and bb with sizes kk and ll, and we know that some ai=xai=x. Notice that this means that i−1i−1 of the x−1x−1 elements before xx have to be in aa, and all the others must be in bb. This means that we can go through all placed elements and count the number of ways to position the unplaced ones using binomial coefficients. You can read the code for better understanding.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n).

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

const long long mod = 998244353;
const int MAX = 2e5 + 42;

// Mint template omitted for readability

Mint fact[MAX], inv[MAX];

Mint C(int n, int k) {
    if(n < k || k < 0) return 0;
    return fact[n] * inv[k] * inv[n - k];
}

Mint get(vector<int> p) {
    int n = p.size();

    int pos_1 = -1, pos_n = -1;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if(p[i] == 1) pos_1 = i;
        if(p[i] == n) pos_n = i;
    }
    assert(pos_1 != -1);
    assert(pos_n != -1);

    if(pos_1 > pos_n) {
        reverse(p.begin(), p.end());
        pos_1 = n - 1 - pos_1;
        pos_n = n - 1 - pos_n;
    }

    vector<vector<int>> g(n + 1);
    if(pos_1 % 2 == pos_n % 2) {
        for(int i = (pos_1 & 1 ^ 1); i < n; i += 2) {
            if(i >= 2) g[i - 2].push_back(i);
        }
        for(int i = pos_1 - 2; i >= 0; i -= 2) {
            g[i + 2].push_back(i);
        }
        for(int i = pos_1 + 2; i <= pos_n; i += 2) {
            g[i - 2].push_back(i);
        }
        for(int i = pos_n + 2; i < n; i += 2) {
            g[i].push_back(i - 2);
        }
    }
    else {
        for(int i = pos_1 - 2; i >= 0; i -= 2) {
            g[i + 2].push_back(i);
        }
        for(int i = pos_1 + 2; i < n; i += 2) {
            g[i - 2].push_back(i);
        }

        for(int i = pos_n - 2; i >= 0; i -= 2) {
            g[i].push_back(i + 2);
        }
        for(int i = pos_n + 2; i < n; i += 2) {
            g[i].push_back(i - 2);
        }
    }

    if(pos_1 != 0) {
        if(pos_1 % 2 == 0) g[0].push_back(1);
        if(pos_1 % 2 == 1) g[1].push_back(0);
    }
    if(pos_n != 0) {
        if(pos_n % 2 == (n - 1) % 2) g[n - 2].push_back(n - 1);
        if(pos_n % 2 != (n - 1) % 2) g[n - 1].push_back(n - 2);
    }

    g[pos_1].clear();
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if(i == pos_1 || i == pos_n) continue;
        assert(g[i].size() == 1);
        if(g[i][0] == pos_n) g[i].clear();
    }

    vector<int> in_degree(n);
    for(int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
        for(auto to : g[v]) in_degree[to]++;
    }

    vector<pair<int, int>> pos(n + 1);
    int t = 0, cnt = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if(i == pos_1 || i == pos_n || in_degree[i] > 0) continue;

        t++;
        cnt = 0;
        if(t == 1) pos[1] = {t, cnt++};
        int j = i;
        while(1) {
            pos[p[j]] = {t, cnt++};

            if(g[j].empty()) break;
            j = g[j][0];
        }
    }
    pos[n] = {t, cnt};

    Mint ans = 1;
    for(int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        int j = i + 1;
        while(pos[j].first == 0) j++;

        auto [ti, ci] = pos[i]; ci++;
        auto [tj, cj] = pos[j];
        if(ti == 2) ci = i - ci;
        if(tj == 2) cj = j - 1 - cj;

        // At i we have ci in first, at j - 1 we have cj
        // Choose cj - ci from j - i - 1
        ans *= C(j - i - 1, cj - ci);
        i = j - 1;
    }

    return ans;
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> p(n);
    for(auto &i : p) {
        cin >> i;
    }

    Mint ans = get(p);
    cout << ans << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    fact[0] = 1; for(int i = 1; i < MAX; i++) fact[i] = fact[i - 1] * i;
    inv[MAX - 1] = 1 / fact[MAX - 1];
    for(int i = MAX - 1; i; i--) inv[i - 1] = inv[i] * i; assert(inv[0] == 1);
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 38 Good Problem
  • 0 Okay Problem
  • 1 Bad Problem
  • 8 Didn't Solve

2190F - Xor Product

  • Idea: nika-skybytska
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: nifeshe
Hints
Hint 1

Find a formula for S(0,0,k)S(0,0,k)

Hint 2

Find a formula for S(x,x,k)S(x,x,k)

Hint 3

You can assume that xx and x+k−1x+k−1 have no non-zero common prefix. Let pp be the most significant bit 22 of x+k−1x+k−1, a=P−1−xa=P−1−x and b=x+k−1−Pb=x+k−1−P. Then S(x,x,k)=S(0,0,max(a+1, b+1)) ∪ [2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1]S(x,x,k)=S(0,0,max(a+1, b+1)) ∪ [2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1].

Hint 4

Find a formula for S(x,y,k)S(x,y,k)

Hint 5

You can't assume that xx and x+k−1x+k−1 have no common prefix, but try to solve when they don't for now

Hint 6

Denote PxPx, aa and bb as in Hint 3, and similarly PyPy, cc and dd for yy. When Px=Py=PPx=Py=P, the formula is S(x,y,k)=[0,max(f(b,d),f(a,c)]∪[2P−1−max(f(a,d),f(b,c),2P−1]S(x,y,k)=[0,max(f(b,d),f(a,c)]∪[2P−1−max(f(a,d),f(b,c),2P−1]. Solve when Px≠PyPx≠Py.

Hint 7

Assume Px<PyPx<Py. S(x,y,k)=[Py−Px−1−min(f(b,c),Px−1),Py−Px+min(f(a,c),Px−1)]∪S(x,y,k)=[Py−Px−1−min(f(b,c),Px−1),Py−Px+min(f(a,c),Px−1)]∪ [Py+Px−1−min(f(a,d),Px−1),Py+Px+min(f(b,d),Px−1)][Py+Px−1−min(f(a,d),Px−1),Py+Px+min(f(b,d),Px−1)].

Hint 8

Common non-zero prefix doesn't change the size of SS, so in general the formula for size is |S(x,y,k)|=min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px) + min(f(a,d)+f(b,d)+2, 2Px)|S(x,y,k)|=min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px) + min(f(a,d)+f(b,d)+2, 2Px). Use digit dp to solve the problem.

Solution

A few notations:

  • S(x,y,k)=(x+i)⊕(y+j)∣0≤i<k, 0≤j<kS(x,y,k)=(x+i)⊕(y+j)∣0≤i<k, 0≤j<k.
  • S(x,y,k,l)=(x+i)⊕(y+j)∣0≤i<k, 0≤j<lS(x,y,k,l)=(x+i)⊕(y+j)∣0≤i<k, 0≤j<l.
  • [l,r]⊕x=i⊕x∣l≤i≤r[l,r]⊕x=i⊕x∣l≤i≤r.
  • [l1,r1]∗[l2,r2]=i⊕j∣l1≤i≤r1, l2≤j≤r2[l1,r1]∗[l2,r2]=i⊕j∣l1≤i≤r1, l2≤j≤r2 (We'll call this the XOR Cartesian product).

Let's try to find a formula for a general S(x,y,k)S(x,y,k). Consider the simplified case x=yx=y.

If x=y=0x=y=0, we can achieve every XOR value up to the maximum possible XOR. Let pp be the smallest integer such that 2p≥k2p≥k. Then S(0,0,k)=[0, 2p−1]S(0,0,k)=[0, 2p−1].

When x≠yx≠y, notice that xx and x+k−1x+k−1 share some common prefix of bits. Let its length be LL. That prefix is present in all x+ix+i and x+jx+j, so it cancels out in XOR. Therefore, we can zero out the top LL bits and assume ⌊log2x⌋≠⌊log2(x+k−1)⌋⌊log2⁡x⌋≠⌊log2⁡(x+k−1)⌋.

Let p=⌊log2(x+k−1)⌋p=⌊log2⁡(x+k−1)⌋ and P=2pP=2p. Then xx does not have the bit PP set, and we can split: - [x, P−1][x, P−1] - [P, x+k−1][P, x+k−1]

Now write S(x,x,k)S(x,x,k) as the union of XOR-products of these two ranges (there are 3 distinct ones, since cross terms are symmetric).

Define a=(P−1)−x,b=(x+k−1)−Pa=(P−1)−x,b=(x+k−1)−P.

Product A: [P, x+k−1]∗[P, x+k−1][P, x+k−1]∗[P, x+k−1]. All numbers in [P, x+k−1][P, x+k−1] have the bit PP set, so it cancels in XOR. After removing that bit: [P, x+k−1]∗[P, x+k−1]=S(0,0,b+1)[P, x+k−1]∗[P, x+k−1]=S(0,0,b+1). (Here b+1=(x+k−1)−P+1=x+k−Pb+1=(x+k−1)−P+1=x+k−P.)

Product B: [x, P−1]∗[x, P−1][x, P−1]∗[x, P−1]. This is similar to the S(0,0,⋅)S(0,0,⋅) case but taken from the other side. Replace each t∈[x, P−1]t∈[x, P−1] with t⊕(P−1)t⊕(P−1), which equals to (P−1)−t(P−1)−t. Since we XOR both sides, the set of XORs does not change, and we get: [x, P−1]∗[x, P−1]=S(0,0,a+1)[x, P−1]∗[x, P−1]=S(0,0,a+1) (here a+1=(P−1−x)+1=P−xa+1=(P−1−x)+1=P−x).

Product C, the hardest case: [x, P−1]∗[P, x+k−1][x, P−1]∗[P, x+k−1] Apply the same transforms:

  • [x, P−1]→[0, a][x, P−1]→[0, a]

  • [P, x+k−1]→[0, b][P, x+k−1]→[0, b]

But now the “removed masks” do not cancel out, so at the end we must XOR by (P−1)⊕P=2p+1−1(P−1)⊕P=2p+1−1.

So: [x, P−1]∗[P, x+k−1]=S(0,0,a+1,b+1) ⊕ (2p+1−1)[x, P−1]∗[P, x+k−1]=S(0,0,a+1,b+1) ⊕ (2p+1−1).

Let's understand how to find S(0,0,k,l)S(0,0,k,l). Claim: S(0,0,k,l)S(0,0,k,l) is a single interval, or S(0,0,k,l)=[0,f(k−1,l−1)]S(0,0,k,l)=[0,f(k−1,l−1)], where f(n,m)f(n,m) is the largest XOR of 0≤i≤n0≤i≤n and 0≤j≤m0≤j≤m.

Proof

The claim is that the interval is [0,M][0,M], where M=f(k−1, l−1)=max0≤i<k, 0≤j<l(i⊕j)M=f(k−1, l−1)=max0≤i<k, 0≤j<l(i⊕j).

This is true because if we can obtain XOR value CC, we can also obtain C−1C−1. Take the most significant bit where CC and C−1C−1 differ and flip that bit by adjusting either ii or jj (whichever has that bit set), then adjust lower bits as needed. This does not increase ii or jj, so it stays within bounds.

It's not hard to compute f(n,m)f(n,m) in general.

How to compute

Let pp be the index of the most significant set bit in (n & m)(n & m). Then f(n,m)=n | m | (2p−1)f(n,m)=n | m | (2p−1).

Using this, Product C becomes an interval: S(0,0,a+1,b+1)⊕(2p+1−1)=[0, f(a,b)] ⊕ (2p+1−1)=[2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1]S(0,0,a+1,b+1)⊕(2p+1−1)=[0, f(a,b)] ⊕ (2p+1−1)=[2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1].

So, combine the three parts: S(x,x,k)=S(0,0,a+1) ∪ S(0,0,b+1) ∪ [2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1]S(x,x,k)=S(0,0,a+1) ∪ S(0,0,b+1) ∪ [2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1].

This can be simplified to: S(x,x,k)=S(0,0,max(a+1, b+1)) ∪ [2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1]S(x,x,k)=S(0,0,max(a+1, b+1)) ∪ [2p+1−1−f(a,b), 2p+1−1].

Now handle x≠yx≠y similarly. First assume that: xx and x+k−1x+k−1 have no common non-zero prefix bits (after stripping common prefix as before, though in this case it's not obvious that this will not change the set, but we'll handle this later), and similarly for yy.

Let: px=⌊log2(x+k−1)⌋,Px=2pxpx=⌊log2⁡(x+k−1)⌋,Px=2px, py=⌊log2(y+k−1)⌋,Py=2pypy=⌊log2⁡(y+k−1)⌋,Py=2py.

Split into four intervals: - [x, Px−1][x, Px−1], [Px, x+k−1][Px, x+k−1] - [y, Py−1][y, Py−1], [Py, y+k−1][Py, y+k−1]

Define a=(Px−1)−x,b=(x+k−1)−Pxa=(Px−1)−x,b=(x+k−1)−Px, c=(Py−1)−y,d=(y+k−1)−Pyc=(Py−1)−y,d=(y+k−1)−Py.

Now consider the four products:

  1. I1=[x,Px−1]∗[y,Py−1]=[0,f(a,c)]⊕(Px−1)⊕(Py−1)I1=[x,Px−1]∗[y,Py−1]=[0,f(a,c)]⊕(Px−1)⊕(Py−1)

  2. I2=[Px,x+k−1]∗[Py,y+k−1]=[0,f(b,d)]⊕Px⊕PyI2=[Px,x+k−1]∗[Py,y+k−1]=[0,f(b,d)]⊕Px⊕Py

  3. I3=[x,Px−1]∗[Py,y+k−1]=[0,f(a,d)]⊕(Px−1)⊕PyI3=[x,Px−1]∗[Py,y+k−1]=[0,f(a,d)]⊕(Px−1)⊕Py

  4. I4=[Px,x+k−1]∗[y,Py−1]=[0,f(b,c)]⊕Px⊕(Py−1)I4=[Px,x+k−1]∗[y,Py−1]=[0,f(b,c)]⊕Px⊕(Py−1)

Notice that the Px=Py=PPx=Py=P case is trivial, we already almost solved it, and by the same reasoning S(x,y,k)=[0,max(f(b,d),f(a,c)]∪[2P−1−max(f(a,d),f(b,c),2P−1]S(x,y,k)=[0,max(f(b,d),f(a,c)]∪[2P−1−max(f(a,d),f(b,c),2P−1] in this case.

Now WLOG assume Px<PyPx<Py. We have a+b=c+d=k−2a+b=c+d=k−2. Notice that a,b<Pxa,b<Px (since a=Px−1−xa=Px−1−x and Px+b<2PxPx+b<2Px). Also notice that c,d<2Pxc,d<2Px, since max(a,b)≤k2<Pxmax(a,b)≤k2<Px, so k<2Pxk<2Px and c+d=k−2c+d=k−2.

Consider I2=[0,f(b,d)]⊕Px⊕PyI2=[0,f(b,d)]⊕Px⊕Py. Since Px<PyPx<Py, write Px⊕Py=Px+PyPx⊕Py=Px+Py. Also f(b,d)<2Pxf(b,d)<2Px.

  • If f(b,d)<Pxf(b,d)<Px, then I2=[Px+Py, Px+Py+f(b,d)]I2=[Px+Py, Px+Py+f(b,d)]

  • If f(b,d)≥Pxf(b,d)≥Px, then [0,f(b,d)][0,f(b,d)] contains PxPx, and we get: I2=[Py, Py+(f(b,d)−Px)] ∪ [Px+Py, Py+2Px−1]I2=[Py, Py+(f(b,d)−Px)] ∪ [Px+Py, Py+2Px−1].

Now consider I3=[0,f(a,d)]⊕(Px−1)⊕PyI3=[0,f(a,d)]⊕(Px−1)⊕Py. XORing by (Px−1)(Px−1) is just a reversal inside [0,Px−1][0,Px−1] and separately inside [Px,2Px−1][Px,2Px−1].

  • If f(a,d)<Pxf(a,d)<Px, then I3=[Py+(Px−1−f(a,d)), Py+(Px−1)]I3=[Py+(Px−1−f(a,d)), Py+(Px−1)]

  • If f(a,d)≥Pxf(a,d)≥Px, then I3=[Py, Py+(Px−1)] ∪ [Py+(2Px−1−f(a,d)), Py+(2Px−1)]I3=[Py, Py+(Px−1)] ∪ [Py+(2Px−1−f(a,d)), Py+(2Px−1)]

An important observation is that if d≥Pxd≥Px, then f(a,d),f(b,d)≥Pxf(a,d),f(b,d)≥Px, and f(a,d),f(b,d)<Pxf(a,d),f(b,d)<Px otherwise, since a,b<Pxa,b<Px. So:

  • If d≥Pxd≥Px, then I2∪I3=[Py, Py+2Px−1]I2∪I3=[Py, Py+2Px−1]

  • If d<Pxd<Px, then I2∪I3=[Py+Px−1−f(a,d), Py+Px+f(b,d)]I2∪I3=[Py+Px−1−f(a,d), Py+Px+f(b,d)]

So in all cases, I2∪I3I2∪I3 is exactly one interval, and its length is: |I2∪I3|=min(f(a,d)+f(b,d)+2, 2Px)|I2∪I3|=min(f(a,d)+f(b,d)+2, 2Px).

The other two intervals are similar, except now we XOR by (Py−1)(Py−1) instead of PyPy. We can rewrite this as (Px−1)⊕(Py−1)=(Px⊕Py)⊕(Px−1)⊕(Py−1)=(Py−2Px)⊕Px(Px−1)⊕(Py−1)=(Px⊕Py)⊕(Px−1)⊕(Py−1)=(Py−2Px)⊕Px.

From this we can get a similar result:

  • If c≥Pxc≥Px, I1∪I4=[Py−2Px, Py−1]I1∪I4=[Py−2Px, Py−1]

  • If c<Pxc<Px, I1∪I4=[Py−Px−1−f(b,c), Py−Px+f(a,c)]I1∪I4=[Py−Px−1−f(b,c), Py−Px+f(a,c)]

So again we get exactly one interval, and |I1∪I4|=min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px)|I1∪I4|=min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px).

Notice that the two cases never intersect:

  • I1∪I4I1∪I4 is inside [Py−2Px, Py−1][Py−2Px, Py−1].

  • I2∪I3I2∪I3 is inside [Py, Py+2Px−1][Py, Py+2Px−1].

Therefore |S(x,y,k)|=|I1∪I4|+|I2∪I3|=min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px) + min(f(a,d)+f(b,d)+2, 2Px)|S(x,y,k)|=|I1∪I4|+|I2∪I3|=min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px) + min(f(a,d)+f(b,d)+2, 2Px)

Remember that we had assumed that xx and x+k−1x+k−1 had no common non-zero prefix bits (and same for yy). If they do, let XX be that common prefix value for xx and YY for yy. Then every element of S(x,y,k)S(x,y,k) is XORed by X⊕YX⊕Y.

However, notice that X,Y≥2PxX,Y≥2Px, since PxPx is the first bit that differs in xx and x+k−1x+k−1, and if we XOR by a bit after PxPx the values will just shift, but the size won't change. So when xx and x+k−1x+k−1 have a common prefix, we can just get rid of it without changing the size of SS.

Now we can solve the original problem, where xx is fixed. If Px=PyPx=Py the size of always smaller than when not, so we only have to consider the case when Px≠PyPx≠Py. In fact, it is always optimal to have Py>PxPy>Px, because having a smaller PyPy will trigger the 2Px2Px part of the formula.

We have aa and bb fixed from xx, so the formula we need to optimize is: min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px) + min(f(a,k−2−c)+f(b,k−2−c)+2, 2Px)min(f(a,c)+f(b,c)+2, 2Px) + min(f(a,k−2−c)+f(b,k−2−c)+2, 2Px) over all 0≤c≤k−20≤c≤k−2. We can easily do that with digit dp in O(logk)O(log⁡k) with a constant factor of around 100100, which solves the problem.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

template <typename T> inline bool umax(T &a, const T &b) { if(a < b) { a = b; return 1; } return 0; }

const long long inf = 4.1e18;
const int LG = 61;

long long get_different(long long power, long long k, long long A) {
    long long B = k - A;
    vector<vector<long long>> dp(2, vector<long long>(16, -inf));
    dp[0][0] = 0;

    int cnt_states = 0;
    for(int L = LG - 1; ~L; L--) {
        vector<vector<long long>> ndp(2, vector<long long>(16, -inf));

        for(int C = 0; C < 2; C++) {
            for(int cd = 0; cd < 4; cd++) {
                int a = A >> L & 1;
                int b = B >> L & 1;
                int c = cd >> 1;
                int d = cd & 1;

                int new_C = (k >> L & 1) ^ c ^ d;
                if((new_C + c + d >= 2) != C) continue;

                for(int mask = 0; mask < 16; mask++) {
                    if(dp[C][mask] < 0) continue;

                    // ac - 0, ad - 1, bc - 2, bd - 3
                    int new_mask = mask;
                    long long add = 0;

                    if((1ll << L) >= power && c) add += (2ll << L) - 2, new_mask |= (1 << 0) | (1 << 2);
                    if((1ll << L) >= power && d) add += (2ll << L) - 2, new_mask |= (1 << 1) | (1 << 3);

                    if((new_mask >> 0 & 1 ^ 1) && (a & c)) add += (1ll << L + 1) - 1, new_mask |= 1 << 0;
                    if((new_mask >> 1 & 1 ^ 1) && (a & d)) add += (1ll << L + 1) - 1, new_mask |= 1 << 1;
                    if((new_mask >> 2 & 1 ^ 1) && (b & c)) add += (1ll << L + 1) - 1, new_mask |= 1 << 2;
                    if((new_mask >> 3 & 1 ^ 1) && (b & d)) add += (1ll << L + 1) - 1, new_mask |= 1 << 3;

                    if(new_mask >> 0 & 1 ^ 1) add += ((long long) (a | c)) << L;
                    if(new_mask >> 1 & 1 ^ 1) add += ((long long) (a | d)) << L;
                    if(new_mask >> 2 & 1 ^ 1) add += ((long long) (b | c)) << L;
                    if(new_mask >> 3 & 1 ^ 1) add += ((long long) (b | d)) << L;

                    cnt_states++;
                    long long cand = dp[C][mask] + add;
                    umax(ndp[new_C][new_mask], cand);
                }
            }
        }

        dp = ndp;
    }

    long long best = -inf;
    for(int mask = 0; mask < 16; mask++) {
        umax(best, dp[0][mask]);
    }
    return best + 4;
}

long long get(long long x, long long k) {
    if(k == 1) return 1;

    int px = __lg(x ^ x + k - 1);
    long long Px = 1ll << px;
    x &= Px * 2 - 1;

    long long a = Px - 1 - x;
    return get_different(Px, k - 2, a);
}

void solve() {
    long long k, x;
    cin >> x >> k;

    cout << get(x, k) << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 14 Good Problem
  • 0 Okay Problem
  • 5 Bad Problem
  • 6 Didn't Solve

2190G - Maximize Determinant

  • Idea: nifeshe
  • Solution: nifeshe
  • Editorial: nifeshe
Hints
Hint 1

|det(A)|≤1|det⁡(A)|≤1 for all AA and X=1X=1 for all nn. Find an easy way to calculate the determinant.

Hint 2

Try to determine when the determinant is 00. Try to calculate it mod2mod⁡2.

Hint 3

Add an extra column to the matrix and look at the difference array for each row.

Hint 4

Since each row has exactly 22 ones now, the determinant is 00 iff there's a cycle in a graph with n+1n+1 vertices and edges (li,ri+1)(li,ri+1). In other words, the determinant is non-zero when the graph is a tree.

Hint 5

Determine if the determinant is −1−1 or 11. When li=rili=ri for all ii, the determinant is a parity of a permutation, so try to transform it into that form.

Hint 6

Root the tree at n+1n+1, let pvpv be the parent of vertex vv, and f(v)f(v) be the index of the edge (v,pv)(v,pv).

Let kk be the number of vv that pv<vpv<v, and let qq be the permutation with qf(v)=vqf(v)=v. Then det(A)=(−1)k⋅sgn(q)det⁡(A)=(−1)k⋅sgn⁡(q).

Hint 7

For the original problem, first solve when det(A)=−1det⁡(A)=−1. If you change two edges, you can always swap them to negate the sign of the determinant, so it's sufficient to determine for each edge if we can negate the sign by editing it.

Hint 8

Try to change edge v→pvv→pv to u→pvu→pv, where uu is a direct child of vv.

Hint 9

If we replace v→pvv→pv with x→yx→y, where xx is in the subtree of vv and yy is outside of subtree of vv, the determinant is simply multiplied by −1[v>pv]+[x>y]−1[v>pv]+[x>y]. It is sufficient to check two extreme cases there.

Hint 10

If det(A)=0det⁡(A)=0 and there are at least 33 components, the answer is the weight of a transformed MST.

Hint 11

If there are exactly 22 components, apply the same logic as in the solution for −1−1. In particular, there's a cycle and we have to change an edge in it to connect two components.

Solution

First let's find an easy way to calculate the determinant of a fixed AA. We can easily see that det(A)∈[−1,0,1]det⁡(A)∈[−1,0,1] for any AA.

Proof

If there's no ones in the first column, the determinant is 00. Otherwise, let ii be the row with li=1li=1 and riri being as small as possible. We can subtract this row from all others, and then expand on that row using the Cramer's rule. This multiplies the determinant by −1−1 or 11 and get rids of one row and one column, so we can apply induction.

Let's first of all understand when it is 00, so in other words we need to find if the determinant is even.

Notice that replacing each row with its difference array is a linear transformation, because we can set ai,j=ai,j−ai,j−1ai,j=ai,j−ai,j−1 for all jj simultaneously, and do that for all ii from the end.

Therefore, let's add an extra column so that the matrix is nn by n+1n+1 now, and replace each row with its difference array. Now every row has exactly one 11 and exactly one −1−1, or exactly two 11's when looked at mod2mod⁡2.

So we have nn binary strings of size n+1n+1 with two 11's in each string and we want to determine if they are linearly independent.

The solution to this is known, and the strings are linearly independent iff there are no cycles in a graph with n+1n+1 vertices and nn edges (xi,yi)(xi,yi).

Therefore, the determinant is 00 iff there is a cycle in a graph with n+1n+1 vertices and nn edges (li,ri+1(li,ri+1) (since each row got replaced with a difference array, so the last 11 shifted to a −1−1 in the next index).

If the graph is a tree, the determinant is 11 or −1−1. Let's learn to determine which one it is.

Notice that if li=rili=ri for all ii, the determinant is the parity of a permutation, so it clearly can't be simplified beyond that, so let's try to get it down to that.

Let's try to transform the matrix into a permutation. Consider the following matrix:

⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢011010000−11−1010000−1000−100−10000⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥[00100−110−10001000−10001−1001−10000]

The tree of this matrix looks like this:

Picture

Denote pvpv as the parent of vertex vv, f(v)f(v) as the index of a row with vv and pvpv. If pv=n+1pv=n+1 holds for all vv, pvpv has a −1−1 and vv has 11 at f(v)f(v)-th row, we are simply in a case of a permutation that was discussed earlier, so let's transform the matrix into that.

Let's make it so pvpv has a −1−1 and vv has 11, so multiply f(v)f(v)-th row by −1−1 if pv<vpv<v:

Picture

Now we can iteratively move pv≠n+1pv≠n+1 to ppvppv by adding f(pv)f(pv) to f(v)f(v). For example, in the picture above for v=4v=4 we add i=1i=1 to i=4i=4 and get a new tree:

Picture

This way we can transform the matrix to have pv=n+1pv=n+1:

Operations
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢01−10−1000011−10−100001000100−10000⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥−→−−−−−−−v=4, r4←r4+r1[00100−110−1000−10001000−1100−110000]→v=4, r4←r4+r1
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢01−10−1000011−10000001000100−100−10⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥−→−−−−−−−v=2, r5←r5+r2[00100−110−1000−10001000010−1−110000]→v=2, r5←r5+r2
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢01−100000011−100−10001000100−100−10⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥−→−−−−−−−v=5, r3←r3+r2[00100−110−1000−10001000010−101−1000]→v=5, r3←r3+r2
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢01000000011−1−10−10001000100−100−10⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥−→−−−−−−−v=1, r2←r2+r1[00100−110−100000−101000010−101−1000]→v=1, r2←r2+r1
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢010000000110−10−10001000100−1−10−10⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥−→−−−−−−−v=3, r3←r3+r1[00100−110000−100−101000010−101−1000]→v=3, r3←r3+r1
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢01000000011000−10001000100−1−1−1−10⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥−→−−−−−−−v=5, r5←r5+r1[00100−110000−100001−100010−101−1000]→v=5, r5←r5+r1
⎡⎣⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢⎢0100000001100000001000100−1−1−1−1−1⎤⎦⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥⎥[00100−110000−100001−100010−101000−1]

At the end we end up with a pv=n+1pv=n+1 for all vv, and since the last column doesn't actually exist in the matrix, the matrix is just a permutation, and we can find its sign.

Notice an imporant fact: during our operations f(v)f(v) didn't change, only pvpv did, so at the end we will always end up with the permutation qq of size nn with qf(v)=vqf(v)=v.

To sum it up, to find the sign of AA:

Let kk be the number of vv that pv<vpv<v, and let qq be the permutation with qf(v)=vqf(v)=v. Then det(A)=(−1)k⋅sgn(q)det⁡(A)=(−1)k⋅sgn⁡(q).

Now let's use this formula to solve the original problem. We have to make AA has a determinant of 11. Obviously, if it's already 11, the answer is 00.

Let's first solve the case when it's −1−1. Notice an important fact: if we modify two rows, we can simply swap them, and that negates the determinant.

So the upperbound on the answer is the sum of two smallest values of aiai. We now simply have to determine the smallest cost of an edge that can negate the determinant on it's own.

Root the tree at n+1n+1 again, we delete the ii-th edge v→pvv→pv, and replace it with an edge x→yx→y, where xx is a direct child of vv. Assume that f(x)=jf(x)=j. How does the determinant change?

Notice that besides ii, only the direction of edge jj changes. In particular, the edge x→vx→v changes to v→xv→x, and ii-th edge is now x→yx→y.

How does qq change? Before the operation, qi=vqi=v and qj=xqj=x, now qi=xqi=x and qj=vqj=v, so it swaps qiqi and qjqj, which negates the sign. Additionally, since jj-th edge was reversed, the value of −1[x>v]−1[x>v] changed to −1[v>x]−1[v>x], so this part was negated as well.

Therefore, the only change in determinant is: −1[v>pv]+[x>y]−1[v>pv]+[x>y]. We can iteratively apply this to get the same result for when xx is not a child of vv.

Therefore, if we replace v→pvv→pv with x→yx→y, where xx is in the subtree of vv and yy is outside of subtree of vv, the determinant is multiplied by −1[v>pv]+[x>y]−1[v>pv]+[x>y].

This means that we can change the sign of determinant if the values of [v>pv][v>pv] and [x>y][x>y] are different.

Let maxvmaxv be the largest value in the subtree of vv, and minvminv the smallest value outside of the subtree of vv. Then we can use the edge v→pvv→pv to change the sign iff maxv>minvmaxv>minv, because if we choose x=vx=v, y=n+1y=n+1, the sign is going to be different compared to when we choose x=maxvx=maxv, y=minvy=minv.

We can use this to determine if we can change the sign with an edge ii and find the smallest cost of one which we can use. This solves the case when the determinant is −1−1.

Now the only thing left is to solve when the determinant is 00. Our graph is not a tree now.

Notice that if there are at least 33 components, we have to change at at least 22 edges, so we don't have to worry about the sign, since we can swap two edges if needed like we did in the previous case.

So we simply have to make the graph a tree while changing a subset of edges with the smallest total cost, which is precisly the MST problem (slightly modified).

The only case left is when the graph consists of two components. We can actually use the exact same observations as in the det(A)=−1det⁡(A)=−1. There's a cycle, we have to remove one edge from it and connect the components. We can also change two edges, but at least one of them has to be in the cycle.

In the first case we have to check if the smallest value in the component with n+1n+1 is smaller than the largest value in the other component, and if so, we can choose any edge on the cycle. Otherwise, we have to apply the same logic as we did earlier.

Final time complexity: O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) due to sorting for finding MST.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

const long long inf = 1e18;

struct DSU {
    int n;
    vector<int> p, siz;
    int comp;

    DSU(int N) {
        n = N - 1;
        comp = 0;
        p = vector<int>(n + 1);
        siz = vector<int>(n + 1);
    };

    void create(int x) {
        p[x] = x;
        siz[x] = 1;
        comp++;
    }

    void init() {
        comp = 0;
        for(int v = 1; v <= n; v++) create(v);
    }

    int get(int x) {
        return p[x] = (p[x] == x? x : get(p[x]));
    }

    bool unite(int x, int y) {
        x = get(x), y = get(y);
        if(x == y) return 0;
        if(siz[x] < siz[y]) swap(x, y);
        comp--;
        siz[x] += siz[y];
        p[y] = x;
        return 1;
    }
};

int get_cycles(vector<int> p) {
    int n = p.size();

    vector<int> used(n);
    int cnt = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if(used[i]) continue;

        int j = i;
        while(!used[j]) {
            used[j] = 1;
            j = p[j];
        }

        cnt++;
    }

    return cnt;
}

long long solve_zero(vector<array<int, 3>> a) {
    int n = (int) a.size();
    int N = n + 1;
    vector<vector<array<int, 3>>> g(N + 1);
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        auto [u, v, w] = a[i];
        g[u].push_back({v, w, i});
        g[v].push_back({u, w, i});
    }
    vector<pair<int, int>> parent(N + 1);
    vector<int> used(N + 1), d(N + 1);

    int extra_edge = -1;
    function<void(int, int)> dfs = [&](int v, int p) {
        used[v] = 1;
        for(auto [to, w, idx] : g[v]) {
            if(to == p) continue;
            if(used[to]) {
                extra_edge = idx;
                continue;
            }

            parent[to] = {v, idx};
            d[to] = d[v] + 1;
            dfs(to, v);
        }
    };
    dfs(N, 0); dfs(1, 0);

    assert(extra_edge != -1);
    parent[1] = {N, extra_edge};

    int sign = 1;
    vector<int> p(n);
    for(int v = 1; v <= n; v++) {
        auto [u, idx] = parent[v];
        p[idx] = v - 1;
        if(v > u) sign *= -1;
    }
    int cnt_cycles = get_cycles(p);
    if((n - cnt_cycles) & 1) sign = -sign;

    long long ans = inf;
    int best_idx = extra_edge;
    if(sign == 1) ans = min(ans, (long long) a[extra_edge][2]);

    int v = a[extra_edge][0];
    int u = a[extra_edge][1];
    if(d[v] < d[u]) swap(u, v);

    int prev = u;
    while(v != u) {
        auto [par, idx] = parent[v];

        // Delete (v, p)
        // Add (v, prev)
        sign *= -1;
        if(v > par) sign *= -1;
        if(v > prev) sign *= -1;
        if(sign == 1) ans = min(ans, (long long) a[idx][2]);

        if(a[idx][2] < a[best_idx][2]) best_idx = idx;

        prev = v;
        v = par;
    }

    long long two_operations = inf;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if(i != best_idx) two_operations = min(two_operations, (long long) a[i][2]);
    }
    two_operations += a[best_idx][2];
    ans = min(ans, two_operations);

    return ans;
}

long long get(vector<array<int, 3>> a) {
    int n = (int) a.size();

    int N = n + 1;
    for(auto &[l, r, w] : a) r++;

    auto edges = a;
    for(auto &[u, v, w] : edges) {
        swap(u, w);
    }
    sort(edges.rbegin(), edges.rend());

    DSU dsu(N + 1);
    dsu.init();
    long long ans = 0;
    for(auto [w, u, v] : edges) {
        if(!dsu.unite(u, v)) ans += w;
    }

    if(dsu.comp > 1) {
        if(dsu.comp > 2) return ans;
        else {
            int cnt_other = 0, max_other = 0;
            for(int v = 1; v <= N; v++) {
                if(dsu.get(N) != dsu.get(v)) {
                    cnt_other++;
                    max_other = max(max_other, v);
                }
            }

            if(cnt_other < max_other) return ans;
            return solve_zero(a);
        }
    }

    vector<vector<array<int, 3>>> g(N + 1);
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        auto [u, v, w] = a[i];
        g[u].push_back({v, w, i});
        g[v].push_back({u, w, i});
    }
    vector<pair<int, int>> parent(N + 1);
    vector<int> sz(N + 1, 1), max_dp(N + 1), weight(N + 1);

    function<void(int, int)> dfs = [&](int v, int p) {
        max_dp[v] = v;

        for(auto [to, w, idx] : g[v]) {
            if(to == p) continue;

            parent[to] = {v, idx};
            weight[to] = w;
            dfs(to, v);
            sz[v] += sz[to];
            max_dp[v] = max(max_dp[v], max_dp[to]);
        }
    };
    dfs(N, 0);

    int sign = 1;
    vector<int> p(n);
    for(int v = 1; v <= n; v++) {
        auto [u, idx] = parent[v];
        p[idx] = v - 1;
        if(v > u) sign *= -1;
    }
    int cnt_cycles = get_cycles(p);
    if((n - cnt_cycles) & 1) sign = -sign;

    ans = inf;
    if(sign == 1) ans = 0;
    else {
        reverse(edges.begin(), edges.end());
        ans = edges[0][0] + edges[1][0];

        for(int v = 1; v < N; v++) {
            if(sz[v] < max_dp[v]) ans = min(ans, (long long) weight[v]);
        }
    }

    return ans;
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<array<int, 3>> a(n);
    for(auto &[l, r, w] : a) {
        cin >> l >> r >> w;
    }

    long long ans = get(a);
    cout << ans << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int ttt = 1;
    cin >> ttt;
    while(ttt--) {
        solve();
    }
}

Xellos
2 months ago, show (+4) #
»
Xellos
2 months ago, hide # |
 
+48
solution for D

The Prufer vertex is the first vertex on the path from NN to N−1N−1. If NN and N−1N−1 are in the same tree, this vertex is fixed and the count is given in the statement. From now on, we may assume they're in different trees.

Let's suppose that we chose the layout of the final tree and almost all endpoints of edges we need to add — all except the last vertex on the path to N−1N−1 that's in the initial tree containing NN. If this vertex is NN, the Prufer vertex isn't in this initial tree. Otherwise it is in this initial tree and there are [size of subtree of vv if it's rooted at NN] ways to choose it so that the Prufer vertex would be vv. Since we know the total count, we can also calculate the count for each vv (initial neighbour of NN) from these relative rules.

What if the Prufer vertex is elsewhere? Let's say we chose only that first added edge on the path from NN to N−1N−1: from NN to vv. The same reasoning above (treating the tree of vv as if it was joined to NN by vv on the input) gives us the number of ways to make vv the Prufer vertex if vv isn't in the initial tree of N−1N−1. Turns out it doesn't depend on the size of the initial tree containing vv.

If the Prufer vertex is in the initial tree of N−1N−1, we again get the count directly from the formula in the statement, in case the trees of N−1N−1 and NN were merged on the input.

It's surprisingly nice and doesn't require complex calculations, although precise reasoning can make a problem difficult just as much as calculations.

→ Reply

alt04
2 months ago, show (+3) #

alt04
2 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+10

I am not to sure about it, but actually I am pretty certain, that for 2190B1 — Sub-RBS (Easy Version) you actually only have to check, whether there exists 2 more opening brackets after the first closing bracket and then return string length minus 2.

Theorem the following are equivalent:

(1) There exists a better subsequence of length l-2

(2) There exists a better subsequence

(3) there are more than 2 opening brackets after the first closing bracket

(1) => (2) is trivial

(2) => (3) Let balance(string) be the balance mentioned in the solution. If there is a better subsequence t, that differs at position i, then b:= balance(t[0:i]) = 2 + balance(s[0:i]) since a ")" was replaced by a "(". To still be regular there must be b closing brackets after i in t, since all these b closing brackets also must have been in s, for s to end with a balance of 0, there must be 2 more opening brackets in s after position i.

(3) => (1) Lets construct a string of len(s)-2 that is a better subsequence than s in the following manner: There are 2 opening brackets after the first closing bracket. let the first of these be at position i. and the second at position j. t = s[0:i-1] + s[i:j] + s[j+1:] so, we only delete the first closing bracket that is directly followed by a opening bracket and the first (or actually any opening bracket) that isnt this first opening bracket at position i. Since the closing bracket was deleted before the opening bracket, the balance remains greater than zero at each position and reaches zero in the end.

Hence (1)=>(2)=>(3)=>(1)

→ Reply

    n = int(input())

      Yes my logic was the same for both parts of Div2 D — the maximum possible len will be n-2 for the subsequence(which can be explicitly constructed in the favourable case like you mentioned). And if the )( condition is not enforced for atleast one index then "(" is basically leading in the string s at all times so you cannot remove any brackets such that "(" comes before ")" in a regular subsequence.

0

Bro, i got a n^2 solution of div2 D2. We can say that we will count a subsequence if it has ")((" as subsequence. Now we can use 4 arrays to track states of the above subsequence. We can make each of them as a n length array of pair where first element of pair represents the count of element and the second element represent the sum of length of subsequencestill now. Here we are using the array of length n as it represents the stack value (count of open bracket — count of close bracket) for those subsequences. Now for each element in s, we can try to move to further state by picking that element or we can stay in same state. Answer will be the sum of subsequence on zero stack value in final state. Check my submission for more clarity

→ Reply
»
Ashwanth.K
2 months ago, show #
»
Ashwanth.K
2 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+30

Problem B2 can also be solved in O(N2)O(N2)

Solution

Main Observation:

  • The length parameter of DP, can be brought down to answer (on fly — while picking the elements), having number of good sequences as part of dp return value, helps.
  • dp[i][b][k]dp[i][b][k] Returns a pair (sum of all lengths of good sequences , no of good subsequences)
  • Sum of lengths can be recalculated as follows:
  • If current idx picked, +1 is added to every good subsequence, So sum of lengths gets added by (number of good sequences in transition)
    • dp[i][b][k].firstdp[i][b][k].first += dp[i+1][b′][k′].firstdp[i+1][b′][k′].first
    • dp[i][b][k].firstdp[i][b][k].first += dp[i+1][b′][k′].seconddp[i+1][b′][k′].second
  • If current idx not picked, sum of lengths is not affected by the current index, so just:
    • dp[i][b][k].firstdp[i][b][k].first += dp[i+1][b][k].firstdp[i+1][b][k].first

358385878

→ Reply

    Bro bob can only win if there is no ones before zeros. In that case Alice can't do anything. If there was some ones before zeros then for each 1 from front select a zero from the end. Solve it by two pointers.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Draunger
      2 months ago, show (+1) #

      Draunger
      2 months ago, hide # ^ |

      Bro can u proof?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        alt04
        2 months ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        alt04
        2 months ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        0

        yes.

        If there is no ones before zeros, every sequence Alice can choose to have the characters non increasing must have the characters monotone and as such also non decreasing, so the reordering doesnt change the bitstring and Alice cant make a valid move.

        In every other case alice can finish the game in one move, by picking the indices of the first k 1s and the last k zeros, such that k is the biggest k such that the kth-first 1 (index i) is still before the kth-last zero (index j). this results in the first k 1s to be replaced by 0s and the last k 0s to be replaced by 1. As such, there is only 0s before index i and only 1s after index j. Inbetween index i and j, there can be no 1 left of any 0 (or otherwise k isnt maximal), so the whole string is now sorted.

        → Reply

Wax
2 months ago, show (+1) #
»
Wax
2 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
0 

~~~~~
D2 was literally  state finding problem of dynamic programming.In D1,We are all about to
find that it is possible to get n-2 iff for a close bracket there is 2 opening bracket after that!

In D2,we have to find the sum of all answers of regular subsequece of the give irregular
sequence.So what things we need must?

1. Current position 
2.current sum which can give info iff the subsequence is regular
3.size of the subsequence 
4.number of opening  bracket following the closing bracket.we will store only at most 2
5.boolian for a close bracket

Move:We will go knapsack for every position either taking it or not taking it.sum will be increased or decreased following the bracket type.
If we get a close bracket,and previously not taken a close,we will take it and mark the boolian true...else normal move.Then if the bracket is open,
We will check if we got a close in previous through boolian!If it is then we will increase the counter of opening bracket else else do normal move....and every time do a not taking move....at last return max(0,n-2) iff boolean is true and counter>=2.
[submission:358392558]

~~~~~

→ Reply

GuptaDivyansh26

GuptaDivyansh26

0

Can anyone explain the solution to Sub-RBS(Easy)?

Moreover, why will counting the max no of "()" pairs not be correct? If maxPairs > first occurence of ')' in s, then shouldn't the ans be 2 * maxPairs?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    fatespeaker
    2 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    fatespeaker
    2 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +5

    Correct me if I misunderstood, but it is an RBS. For every '((', there is a corresponding '))', so the number of non-overlapping "()()" pairs will always be n2n2. Instead, we want to delete two values (which must be alike to keep RBS nature). We can do this by deleting the earliest occurrence of '))' and then deleting a later occurrence of '((' as long as there is another '((' later in the string. This works because we will still have an equal number of open and closed brackets, and it will not cause the first or last bracket to be facing the wrong way. Ensuring another '((' bracket exists is beneficial to ensure that the sequence fits the constraints. This is because all brackets after the first deleted will be shifted over to the left, so it will be guaranteed that some '((' bracket will be shifted into the location that was previously occupied by a '))' bracket.

    Hope this helps! (Slightly simplified approach than editorial for D1, but this is much simpler and keeps the same idea. It is described in the beginning of the editorial for D2.)

    → Reply
»

My solution to problem B is to count the frequency of each number's occurrence. Find the global MEX value m. Check every number between 0 and m-1: if any number only appears once, output YES. If all numbers occur>=2 times: if m<=1->output NO. Otherwise ->output YES.

It seems like checking if position of first closing bracket is less or equal to n/2−1n/2−1 is sufficient to determine whether RBS has better subsequence of length n−2n−2 in B1. See submission: 358423397. Using this fact, one can come up with dp[i][l][b][p]dp[i][l][b][p] for B2 – number of bracket subsequences on prefix ii with length ll, balance bb and position of first closing bracket pp (if there is no closing bracket, p=0p=0). Transitions are fairly easy to derive and to calculate final answer just iterate through RBS length ll and first closing bracket position pp, if p<=l/2−1p<=l/2−1 add (l−2)∗dp[n][l][0][p](l−2)∗dp[n][l][0][p] to the answer. Submission: 358443056.

+3

can anybody explain why this extremely simple code gets accepted on 2190B1 — Sub-RBS (Easy Version)?

Somehow if the there is a ')' in the first half -1 (so n/2-1) of characters, the answer is n-2, else it is -1.

Is it just luck, or is there a reason behind it?

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int t,i,y,m,n,cnt,rsp,pr;
char v[200005];
 
int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
 
    
    cin>>t;
 
    while(t--){
        cin>>n;
        cin>>v;
        int k=0;
        cnt=rsp=pr=0;
        
        for(i=0;i<n;i++)
            if(v[i]==')' && i+1<n/2){
                rsp=n-2;
                break;
            }
        
        if(rsp==0)cout<<-1;
        
        else cout<<rsp;
        
        cout<<'\n';
    }
    
}
→ Reply

    +3

    as prooven and not yet disprooven in my comment you gotta return l-2 if and only if after the first closing bracket there are gonna be 2 more opening brackets in the string else return -1

    this is equivalent to:

    (4) there is a ")" in the first n/2-1 characters

    argumentation: there are exactly n/2 opening brackets in each regular bracket sequence.

    (4) There is a ")" in the first n/2-1 characters <=> The first ")" is in the first n/2-1 characters <=> there are only n/2-2 "(" before the first ")" <=> there are atleast 2 "(" after the first ")" (1)

0

Hello everyone, I would greatly appreciate someone telling me what am I doing wrong in the easy version of problem B. My idea is the following:

Let's say we have regular string s of brackets. Since s is regular we have the following sequence: a1,b1,a2,b2,…,an,bna1,b1,a2,b2,…,an,bn. aiai represents number of consecutive '(' and bibi of consecutive ')'. If ∑ni=2ai>1∑i=2nai>1, then we can construct regular subsequence of ss by removing the last ')' in b1b1, and removing any '(', that is not the first '(' in a2a2. If the sum condition does not hold we have two possible situations:

1 — a1,b1,1,b2a1,b1,1,b2

2 — a1,b1a1,b1, where a1=b1a1=b1. Here I also highlight that the condition on prefixes holds!

The second case trivially does not have greater subsequence! Now for the first case we see that we have to keep the first a1a1 '(' since those exist in original string ss, but then we have to remove the last '(', and subsequently another ')' from either b1b1 or b2b2. But then we will share the prefix a1a1 followed by a1a1 ')', obviously this string would be either a prefix of ss or would be smaller as we would have ')' in subsequence where '(' would be in original sequence.

Now my question is, is there anything that I am not seeing correctly here? As my solution would yield n−2n−2 solution in case the sum condition holds, which would maximize the length of subsequence, and I think I have proven that this condition is both sufficient and necessary. Also I am linking my implementation for the possibility of implementation being incorrect. 

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve()
{
    int n;
    string s;
    cin >> n >> s;

    int l = 0;
    int o = 1;

    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++)
    {
        if (s[i] == ')' && o == 1)
            o = 0;
        if (s[i] == '(' && o == 0)
            ++l;
    }

    if (l > 1)
        cout << n - 2 << endl;
    else
        cout << -1 << endl;
}

int main()
{
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--)
        solve();
}
→ Reply

why my solution is wrong for problem A

void solve()
{
    int n;
    cin>>n;
    vector<int> a(200);

    int color = 1;
    for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
        int elem;
        cin>>elem;
        a[elem] = color;
        if(elem > 1 && a[elem] == a[elem-1]) {
            cout<<"NO"<<endl;
            return;
        }
        if( elem < n && a[elem] == a[elem+1]) {
            cout<<"NO"<<endl;
            return;
        }
        //parity
        if(color == 1) color++;
        else color--;
    }
    cout<<"YES"<<endl;
}
